title: vimeo_1179355438
description: Thursday 2 April 2026 | Environment Committee Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill Hearing of evidence: List of submitters: Waikato River Authority Antoine Coffin, Kaihautū Stu Kneebone, Co-Chair (Crown Appointee) Choose Clean Water Tom Kay, Spokesperson Love and Co. Hamish Firth Ian Munro Selwyn District Council Cr Tracey MacLeod Cr Sophie McInnes Save our Springs Aotearoa New Zealand Inc Kevin Moran, Coordinator John Commissaris, Lawyer Groundswell NZ Jamie McFadden, Spokesperson Bryce McKenzie, Spokesperson Laurie Paterson, Spokesperson
descriptionSummary: Planning Bill. Planning Bill; Natural Environment Bill. List of

chunks.text: Questions. Thank you. Morena koutou, tena koe Madam Chair, members of the committee, tena koutou. My name's Stu Kneebone. I'm one of the co-chairs of the Waikato River Authority. With me is our Kaihautū, CEO, Anton Coffin. So look, thank you very much for this opportunity.

We really appreciate you adding these extra schedules of submitters in. So I'll now hand over to our Kaihautū, Anton, to take you through our submission. Thank you. Good morning, members of the committee. There's a little bit of trepidation being first up in the morning.

You don't get a feel for what people have said right before you and what's been happening and get into it. So, a little bit nervous being very first up, but we'll do our best. Thank you for the opportunity to present this morning. And with your leave, I'll just get straight into our presentation. So today we're going to give a summary of our submissions from the Waikato River Authority. And just as a little bit of background, our authority came into existence in 2010 as a result of the Waikato River treaty settlements.

We're a statutory body that was established with three purposes, one of those being to set the vision and strategy, Te Tiriti Whai Mana, for the Waikato River, promote integrated, coordinated, and holistic approach to the management of the river, and also to fund rehabilitation activities.

So we're part of three settlement acts for the river that cover the whole river from the Huka Falls all the way up to the mouth of the Waikato River. There are also five river iwi post-governance entities that were established as part of the settlements, and all of them have submitted to the bills as part of this process.

As I said before, we're an independent statutory body, and we actually got a really interesting co-governance model where we have five Crown representatives and five river iwi representatives. Today, we're advocating for Te Tiriti Whai Mana, the vision and strategy for the Waikato River. The vision and strategy, Te Tiriti Whai Mana, was intended by Parliament to be a primary direction-setting document for the river.

It's a central element of the unique legislation in New Zealand enacted in relation to the river and its catchment and all it embraces, including the large catchment of the Waipā River as well. Our primary interest in the bill is to ensure there's no diminishment of the status and effect of Te Tiriti Whai Mana or the role of the River Authority in the management of the Waikato and Waipā River catchments.

Just as a slight aside, we're not technically a post-settlement governance entity. We were set up as a unique body to be independent, but also to have roles in collaboration and partnership with the Crown and also with river iwi. In terms of some context, as you know, the Waikato catchment is a big catchment. It's over a million hectares.

There are longstanding relationships of the iwi and hapū with the lands and waters. But also, today, farming is a really important part of the regional economy. Hamilton and Taupō are urban and industrial growth areas and transport hubs. And there are many fast-track listed projects and referrals in this area.

So it just sort of talks to people wanting to be in this area. There's legacy electricity generation, and long-term municipal takes, both for Waikato and Auckland. So the river is very, very important to lots of parts of our area. Over the last 15 years since our inception, we've made a concerted effort in restoration, planning, and implementation for the long-term restoration and health and wellbeing of the Waikato River.

As I've mentioned before, you would have heard from river iwi talking to their special relationships and connections they have. As drafted, we feel that the bills do not uphold the existing treaty settlement arrangements, particularly in relation to the Waikato and Waipā Rivers. For the most part, the bills are silent.

In the present system, there are lots of ways that the vision and strategy, Te Tiriti Whai Mana, connects. These include when preparing and reviewing regional policy statement, it must be included. And it avoids the Schedule 1 process, so it gets in as a matter of right. National policy statements and environmental standards, there are direct links to those.

The Te Tiriti Whai Mana directs consistency of plans prepared by councils, and a range of different instruments and tools that make sure that Te Tiriti Whai Mana is at the forefront of all the decisions that have been made in the planning system. As well as some of the more recent advancements in terms of links to wastewater environmental performance standards, and also land use intensification.

One of the really important roles that we also play is in appointing commissioners. So we work quite closely with river iwi to make sure that there's a voice for those people who have a really close connection in some of the large-scale applications, and that's a process that's been working successfully for some time. Turning our thoughts to what changes can be made, we recommend some changes now rather than later.

And we really think that the committee has an opportunity to set up the bills for equivalency. And these are in the areas of the interpretation. Could be adding the settlement acts in Section 9, having references to the Waikato River Authority. We were suggesting a new Section 10A, where you have particular regard and recognition of Te Tiriti Whai Mana up front.

And in the goals, there could be some recognition of treaty settlements. We've turned our minds to some of the drafting when we were preparing our submissions, butThey felt they were very much narrow in terms of what our role is, understanding that the treaty settlements are a package, and they need to be approached in a concerted and comprehensive way. And so even though we'd put our mind to lots of the drafting, they needed to also consider what kind of impacts they might have on other parts of the settlement that are also important.

So what are we requesting? We're asking that the committee change the clauses to refer to equivalency, and these are in clauses nine and 10 in each of the bill. We're thinking that relying entirely on the post-enactment process to retrofit the treaty settlement arrangement is an unnecessary risk. And we recommend the process for secondary legislation waits for the discussions to be had with our PSGE partners in the catchment.

I certainly, and some of the committee members might remember when the RMA came in, and it was quite exciting around the hierarchy that was going to be put in, and we'd have national policy statements, we'd have regional policy statements, we'd have regional plans, we'd have district plans, and the hierarchy from the legislation would cascade. But it took two generations to achieve it.

As many will know, the transitional plans took 10, 15 years. Regional policy statements come after, then regional plans, and then the national policy statements. So we did it the wrong way around, and it seems like they're learning from that. This time we could do it the right way around, getting the legislation right, getting the secondary legislation right, and then it cascades down.

Just to conclude, we certainly have, you would have seen from the tenor of our submission that we're really concerned about retrofitting the current treaty settlement arrangements after the bills are enacted and the secondary legislation and plans are already in play. And we're asking that the committee recommend that existing settlements are discussed now in the new system of secondary legislation.

Certainly, we want to be included in the discussions and the formulation of the bills as they move through the legislative process, particularly in regards to the purpose and functions of the River Settlement Acts and the roles of the Waipato River Authority. We're thinking there's an opportunity to get things right, right now, that will set us well up for the next few generations.

And just going to stop there and see if there's any questions you may have. Look, thank you very much. We have got one question from Lan. Tena koutou. Thank you so much for your submission. I wanted to pick up on, firstly, just your comments around going first this morning.

We were actually in public hearings for a different bill even earlier this morning, but I wanted to pick up on a number of comments that we've heard from multiple iwi and hapū, both settled and non-settled. And virtually, they talked in different language about their concerns about treaty relationships and what the passing of these bills might do to that, and one of them used the word eviscerate treaty relationships.

You're sounding very pragmatic and constructive in being willing to work in a way where you think-- It sounds like you're keen to get things right now. Do you think simply more time in this process would allow you to work with the Crown, to work with officials, to actually ensure that you're getting it right, right now? Because as you're likely aware, there's very constrained time frames around the government wanting to push this through.

Can you comment on that? Well, first to acknowledge the sentiment that's been given to the select committee. We've come here with a mindset of trying to be really constructive, and finding a way that we can put our best foot forward to make sure the treaty settlement's integrity is reflected in the bills.

And I suppose that doesn't dismiss the sentiment that we're hearing. People are being very disappointed about where are the principles of goodwill and integrity and good faith. Where are the principles that are enshrined in the legislation that was passed by Parliament in terms of the relationship and the partnership with the Crown?

So notwithstanding those things, we're trying to see if there is a possibility with the time that we do have to get things right. So yes, time is an important factor to get things right. You would have seen the benefit of having time in lots of legislation that's passed quickly. You either spend the time when you're preparing it, or you spend the time trying to fix it afterwards. And so it seems like we're in that part of the process. We can make choices. Do we use the time constructively to get things right, or do we not use the time and then we go onto the other side

and you're in a deficit position where you're trying to fix things or not be able to fix things, and then the fallout that comes from those. Mm-hmm. Look, thank you very much for your time today. We have met time. We appreciate your submission. So thank you. Hey, thanks for the opportunity.

Thank you very much. Kia ora. Kia ora. Kia ora. So we now move to our next submitter who is here, Choose Clean Water. Tom, welcome to the select committee this morning. You've got 10 minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you. Cool. Thank you very much. I'm going to focus the things that I speak to just on a couple of issues. You've heard from a lot of submitters already.

Choose Clean Water has a wide concern and mandate to comment on water generally, but I'm going to focus specifically on drinking water protections. Then I'm going to comment briefly on some of the methods that you're looking at to protect things like that.And then I've provided you with a PDF document, which may or may not have made it to you already. But I can just note some extra things we've put in there that you can look at in your own time that I don't need to walk you through.

So I'll try and keep things pretty brief. The first thing I want to talk about or take your minds back to is the Havelock North drinking water Campylobacter outbreak, which you will know very well being from Hawke's Bay. 2016, 5,500 cases of Campylobacter illness, 45 hospitalisations, three deaths connected to that outbreak, $21 million cost to that community, and ultimately an issue with the drinking water system, with the protection of the source all the way through to water coming out of people's taps.

Now, this is a massive issue or a growing issue across the country. In some ways, unfortunately, what was the biggest case recorded in the world is potentially at risk of being a small case in the scheme of things. We've had Selwyn spending $5 million on investigations to secure their drinking water supply with a potential $400 million long-term cost. Gore has just built a new pipe under one of their rivers to dilute their water because it was contaminated with nitrates above drinking water standards. Sorry, Waimate was. Gore and

Waimate are both building pipes to dilute their water supplies. And then there's some estimates around places like Christchurch, if their water was contaminated beyond drinking water standards, that it would cost in the order of hundreds of millions into the billions of dollars to deal with their water. After the Havelock North drinking water Campylobacter outbreak, there was a report written, an inquiry in 2017, and I've put some pages from that in a document in front of you.

And I want to draw your attention to a couple of conclusions in that. Paragraph 617 in particular, the inquiry considers it essential that the protection of drinking water sources be expressly recognised in the primary resource management legislation. So for a government that has reassured us that it's going to be evidence-based, you have an opportunity here to take a recommendation from the past and act on that in your resource management legislation that you're drafting at the moment.

The inquiry goes on to recommend things like amending Section 6, so essentially making drinking water protection a matter of national importance, to provide regional councils with explicit direction to protect drinking water sources, and basically to say, if you don't do this stuff, you need some other form of national direction that does that. Now, you might say, "Hey, we did that.

We've got a national environmental standard for drinking water. We've got an NPS for freshwater." The NPS for freshwater does prioritise human drinking water under Te Mana o te Wai, but the government is planning or proposing to scrap that hierarchy. And the bills you have in front of you, the Natural Environment and Planning Bills, only say that basically, we need to have regard to drinking water supplies. They do not direct the protection of drinking water supplies or drinking water sources being rivers, lakes, aquifers as a matter of national importance.

So I think that's a huge gap in the bill where we risk significantly degrading our drinking water supplies or essentially having this continue to be missed 10 years after you were given these recommendations, and ultimately cost our communities in terms of illness, but also financially. Moving on from that to, and you can obviously get advice on that as you wish, and I recommend you do, but yeah, some strong recommendations there that I think you should pay attention to.

Moving on from that to some of the tools that you're looking at using to protect things like that, regardless of whether you decide that you should only have regard to drinking water sources or whether you should actually protect them as a matter of national importance. There have been a lot of comments from submitters on the tools that you're going to use. Some that have struck me have been around farm plans and non-regulatory mechanisms. Environment Canterbury, for example, sat in front of you and said, "Hey, look, farm plans are working quite well in our

region." In their view, a lot of people have A grades. I want to draw your attention to page five of the documents I've given you, which is the Ōtūwharekai Ashburton Lakes report written by Ministry for the Environment. I suggest you get some advice on this too. But some comments I've highlighted there basically point out to you that farm plans generally in these places have not been connected to outcomes. So it's all good for Environment Canterbury to get here and sit in front of you and say that a lot of their farm plans have A grades.

It doesn't matter if they have an A grade if nothing in that farm plan is connected to the water quality outcome for the place that we're trying to protect. So the report specifically says, the farm environment plan system is overly focused on process rather than environmental outcomes. So long as farm environment plan A or B grades were achieved, intensification of high-risk areas near the Ashburton and Ōtūwharekai Lakes were not monitored or controlled. So these lakes continued to degrade despite the fact that you had a supposedly robust,

non-regulatory system in place. And if you go to the final page of that report that I've put in there or page seven in my document, it says the new National Freshwater Farm Plan system has similarities to the ECAN system, and that hasn't changed. This report's from a few years ago, but the system that you are looking at is ultimately going to be similar. There's other comments in there around lack of information and transparency.

Environment Canterbury has these plans across the region. They don't have copies of them. They don't know how many cows are operating across all these catchments. They don't know how much fertilizer is being used across all these catchments. They have no information readily available to them on the pressures in these catchments, and that's another issue that the report pointed out. So I just want you to be aware that you're being told that this non-regulatory stuff is going to work. The evidence suggests otherwise.

And I'd also point out that farm plans have been something that's been used in New Zealand since the 1950s for soil conservation and erosion. We've been told for decades that they will solve our problems, and they have not. So just be really aware of that. Finally, I want to draw your attention to a couple of other things that I'll let you look at in your own time, but I've included two other attachments, C and D.

One, I've heard developers sit in front of you and say, "Look, we don't need much regulation. It's all red tape. Why would we build under a cliff face? Why would we build in a floodplain? We're not that stupid."A couple of cases here where that's happened. Tangoyo and Hawke's Bay, a developer actually pushed council for a private plan change to allow them to develop in a floodplain. It's since been zoned as basically a red zone.

Have a look at that story or get some advice on it. Another example from Matarangi, where a developer cleared a wetland despite the NPS saying that that's something that you can't do. So due diligence just not done despite their assurances. Finally, we've heard a bit about things like cultural values supposedly getting in the way of resource consents. It's been something that's come through quite strongly from Federated Farmers, for example.

I've just included part of the Gore District Plan decision, which basically says that in that region, in that plan, those concerns are unfounded. This doesn't get in the way of day-to-day farming activities. So have a look at that, get some advice on that. And that's about seven minutes, so maybe if you've got a question or two, I've got time for them. Thank you, Tom, for that submission. We'll go to Rachel first.

Great management of your submission time, too. Yes. Thank you for that. And you've got a lot in the submission about limits and the workarounds and how they have to be balanced about against other things and the like. So acknowledging that content, but what I'm interested in asking you to talk about is what we hear is if you had a national limit for, say, nitrates, which is what you refer to, then sometimes...

Well, not even nitrates, but for some things, people will say, well, the limits are set at a point that is beyond what a normal environment would have, even if there was no-... human interference going. Yeah. So is there a role for targets or some other mechanisms, or where do you think limits should be set- Yeah... in terms of a pristine environment versus- Yeah. So my view on this is this has been a- This has been a red herring for over 10 years.

I started getting involved in the national policy statement development back in sort of 2017-ish, maybe even earlier than that. Early versions of the national policy statement have always said, if you have a place that naturally exceeds a limit, that's fine, it provides for that. It gives you an exception. The existing 2020 NPS gives you that exception.

So all this rhetoric that we're hearing about streams from pristine environments exceeding limits, it doesn't matter. It's fine. The NPS provides for that. I think there's also some misconceptions about how different New Zealand is as a country. You're often told, "Hey, we've got to go catchment by catchment and come up with limits that are really specific." We're not that special. We're not that different.

New Zealand has these diverse landscapes, but from a freshwater ecology perspective, from freshwater ecologists, they can pretty confidently say, "Look, this is a general limit that we should have across our country." There will be exceptions. There are exceptions provided for in the NPS. But we shouldn't go back through this, we need it to be case by case, and then as soon as we try and do that, we get told that that's unfair, that's different for different regions.

We need it to be a blanket approach. As soon as we do a blanket approach, we're told that's unfair. And it's just you're being led down this path for decades, I think, and it's not that complicated. Just as supplementary to that, what about quite leaky soil? So I've got on my office wall the Greenpeace and Ngāi Tahu map of the South Island, and the nitrates in Southland and Canterbury are very clearly different from Otago.

Yeah. And there's a recent report on drinking water risk in Southland that's come out about those short residence times. I think there is some benefit through things like spatial planning. Southland Plan, for example, looked a lot at the risk profile because of those soils. And I think that's where spatial planning potentially gives you a huge advantage to say what types of activities are suitable on these soils that can then achieve the limits that are set nationally or whatever it is that you end up doing.

So totally scope for that. The thing that we run into the problem with is where, and it's happened in Hawke's Bay, for example, we have some very leaky soils where growing grapes is great, but it requires a huge amount of water. It's sort of this unfortunate consequence of that landowner having ended up with that land and growing grapes on that land and then saying, "Well, I need more water than everyone else because I'm on this old gravel riverbed." And it's sort of like, well, we actually need to spatially plan to say,

is that a suitable activity to be happening on that land because we're wasting a lot of water on that space. So yeah. Okay. Look, Tom, we are going to have to leave it there. We have met time. We appreciate your time and your submission, so thank you. Thank you. Yeah. So we will now move to our next submitter, Love and Co.

And I believe Love and Co.- oh, there we go. Ian and Hamish, welcome to the select committee this morning. We will hand it over to you to talk us through your submission, and then we'll open up for questions. Thank you, and good morning, members. The submission by Love and Co., the director, John Love, was unable to join you today, so he asked Hamish and I to speak to some of the key points.

We are consultants that have done a bit and seen a bit, and when he asked us for some advice when he was preparing his submission, we had a few things to say. So if you're happy, we'll just take you through some of the issues as we see it and look forward to any questions you have. And to dive right into it, and just working through the points that John had made, I think everyone would agree that adding a concept of being solutions focused to the bill is a great idea.

But the challenge that we see is if there's not clarity on what it is councils are being asked to do as consent authorities, what solutions might be focused on is hugely ambiguous. And specifically, if councils are primarily focused in the context of a planning consent there to accept an applicant can make an application for what they want, have it assessed against whether it meets the relevant plan or legal matters, then being solutions focused means don't muck around, don't be obtuse, be constructive, act in good faith, move things forward. And I think everyone would agree with that.

ButBut what Hamish and I sometimes see are council officers thinking they're environmental gatekeepers, and we could show you written examples of Section 92 requests where the solutions focus is to ask applicants to not infringe rules that they're seeking consent for in the first place as a way to solve that problem.

So we would encourage Parliament to think about, well, if we want to solve problems, what is the context of the problem being solved? And if it's about officers thinking it's their role to take an application and shape it as to what they think would be a good outcome, well, then being solutions focused might not help speed things up or help progress things.

If I can take that a step further. John is a developer of housing, multi-unit housing in Auckland, and both Ian and I are consultants with 25 to 30 years' experience each. So we've seen what happens when an act of Parliament is, in a way, interpreted by council in such a way that makes it very, very difficult to get things done.

We're working on a job for John right now that's been in council for over a year. Our solution is that council should not have that ability to make it take that long for what is 50 or 60 houses on a site zoned for housing. Thank you, Hamish. Moving on.

Appreciate that there's a proposal through Section 14 of the bill to exclude certain effects from consideration. And the point we'd make there is that if the intent is to exclude something, it needs to be fully excluded. And the example in John's submission was the difference between landscape effects and a landscape character or landscape values effect, which either needs to be all in or all out, not half in or half out. And the point we'd just make about that again is to say it would be probably negative if positive effects also got excluded from consideration.

Usually trying to find a way to get that betterment is something that a lot of applicants want to do. And it may incentivize them to not be so interested in those things if they can't claim any credit or value from that. And I think we've got to look at this in context. What John is talking about here isn't about destroying some outstanding natural landscape or visual in, say, Central Otago.

We're talking about a highly modified environment of urban Auckland, where a landscape and visual assessment really seems unnecessary given the context of what you're building in. So it's about making sure that we don't overregulate or provide... We've got to provide a pathway for getting things done that we want to be done in an area.

Yes. I'm currently dealing with an application for a high-rise tower in Auckland CBD, subject to an unlimited building height, and there's a request for a landscape assessment of it, which is difficult for people to really follow. One matter we'd also draw your attention to is in proposed Section 38 of the bill, very specific. The way it's written, every permitted activity must be registered, and to be registered, one must include a variety of information, including reports from suitable experts confirming its permitted affected party approvals and so

on. And the likelihood is that a council would then want its own experts to review an applicant's experts. And there's a real risk of making permitted activities much harder than they currently are and almost needing a resource consent to show that you don't need a resource consent. So we'd absolutely commend to you just being clear in what circumstances you want permitted activities to be registered and why.

In principle, it seems to make a bit of sense that everyone can keep track of what people are doing, but there's a big difference between an applicant just providing notice to a council that it wishes to undertake a permitted activity and someone having to seek permission to confirm that what they want to do is in fact permitted in every single instance. And an example I'll give you, rather boring one.

I might want to build a fence in a special character area. The maximum height in a rear or a side fence might be 1.8 meters. So do I have to go and show that I'm getting permission to confirm the permitted activity before I build the fence, or can I build the fence and provide the information later? Or why would there be any need for me to go and get another piece of paper from council to confirm the permitted activity of building a fence to the standard height in that special character area?

It seems to us to be adding paperwork to the process, not taking it away. We have a lot of experience through resource consents or planning approvals, as they may become, and particularly with requests for further information, requests for peer reviews, use of things like specialist urban design panels or design review panels, et cetera.

And I think the point that John's making is not that these things are inappropriate or wrong, but really need to be subject to clearer frameworks and guidelines. The Auckland Urban Design Panel, for instance, is now mandatory, including four to six weeks wait in some cases, and there's just no negotiation about that. Even where an applicant has obtained specialist reports and the council has specialist reports, it still wants that, and there's simply no way out of that framework.

So the suggestion was that those things, as good as they can be, should remain fully voluntary, and for applicants to also wish to be part of, rather than, for instance, spending money on their own consultants if they wish. Related to that, we see a lot, again, the request for further information. We really would support a requirement that any person invited to give a comment or review an application really should be suitably qualified and experienced.

This is a lot of time and money getting second opinions or third opinions or fourth opinions, and really, it should be about who's the most suitably qualified to give that assurance, rather than do you happen to be part of team council or team applicant? Further on that is the independence of some of these boards.

They'll often get confidential council officer briefings beforehand, and it sometimes feels as if you're being tried before a jury where you've got no chance of winning. So we would express that they need to be independent and separate from council in a way so that we can get good outcomes.

Just because a council officer doesn't like the proposal doesn't mean it's got merit, and doesn't mean it shouldn't be approved. And just on that point of independence, we're aware that independent commissioners get widely used. We're aware, especially in the last six months or so, there's been some very well-documented public discussions around the nature and competence of some of those commissioners and how they become commissioners and who appoints them, et cetera.

We would say there's huge support for independent commissioners and the assistance they can provide everyone, whether they're a submitter or a council or an applicant, or just a member of the community wanting confidence in the system. But we would say that where there is a real opacity in the current system is the appointment process of how specific commissioners find themselves appointed in specific instances or not.

And we would absolutely encourage a strengthening of independent commissioner use across the country, including possibly improving the training they receive, possibly helping councils make appointments in a consistent and fully independent way. And in the case of things that aren't public hearings, like making notification decisions, it's really uncertain and unpredictable when a council will let an applicant have an independent commissioner make that decision, and when a council says, "No.

We don't have to, so the answer's no." And it's something we again think could be made much more transparent for everyone's benefit. So on that- Thank you, Ian. I'm just going to jump in there because you have almost met time. And there is a question here, but if you want to potentially wrap it up, if you can, that would be great. Oh, look, the last thing we'll say then is just the planning tribunal idea.

Really support it. I think there's always been a gap between going to a High Court or Environment Court, which is a huge process, and the limitations of the current Section 357 objection process. So something in the middle that can rove around the country providing consistent help seems hugely valuable. We would encourage it to be broadened than it currently is to cover all sorts of issues.

But as you requested, we'll wrap it up. Thank you so much for your time, and good luck to you going forward. Thank you. Yeah, look, we are going to have to wrap it up there because we have met time. But thank you very much, Ian and Hamish, for your submission today from Love and Co. Thank you. Thank you. So moving on now to our next submission is the Selwyn District Council.

Who hopefully are joining us online, coming through. Okay. Good. Welcome to the select committee this morning. We can see you both there. You have 10 minutes with us, so take your submission as read, and then we'll open up for questions once you talk us through it. So over to you.

Thanks for the opportunity to speak today on the Selwyn District Council's written submission. My name is Councillor Tracey McLeod, and with me today is Councillor Sophie McGinness. We support the intent of these bills, but our experience in a high growth district like Selwyn means we've already experienced the gap that opens up between policy intent and what is actually deliverable on the ground. Our core point is that this reform programme runs the risk of becoming another RMA, and some of us are old enough to remember when that came in.

And that goal posts take time to materialize, and then they start to shift year on year, and it's the rate payers who end up paying the price for those shifts. So I'll just pass over to Sophie, who will be making a few comments before you get me again. Yeah. Kia ora koutou. So our first query is around the implementation of these new bills, particularly the timeline and the cost. So for instance, our district plan review, which only completed, was notified a couple of years ago. We're still resolving appeals now.

That started in 2015. And we're worried that knowing the pace of this, even when the zoning and everything else will be largely determined further up the funnel, the speed and potentially poor sequencing of all of the direction could easily result in councils having to stop and start and create poor outcomes. Realistically, the other option of slowing down means that concurrent reforms that are going on elsewhere in the government could then also be impacted. So not quite sure what the answer is.

That's for you guys to work out. But the unclear costs is also huge. Just looking the other day at what the West Coast combined plan so far is closer to $9 million, I believe. But in the meantime, we're starting our long term plan planning soon, which is the budgets. Rating caps are imminent, and somehow we've got to plan for the costs of implementing all of these reforms.

Ideally, for us, the sequencing would be national direction, then environmental limits, then spatial planning, and a period of time that is comparable to the complexity of this job across each region.Our other comment around that is community engagement, just because the funnel is going to be determining a lot of what we have to implement down on the ground. And the new bills will significantly limit opportunities for local input, and engagement and collaboration with Maori is also reduced down to participation.

The Crown can do a lot better than this. You can prevent further loss of trust in public institutions by enabling public local flexibility in how we implement this. And the gap at the moment between public opinion and legal directives, it's like a bellwether. It's not a reason for shutting people out of important decisions in their local areas.

Thanks, Sophie. And so I'm just going to build on those implementation challenges. As I said, our concern is not with the intent, but with how things like spatial plans and land use plans will actually operate in practice. Those spatial plans are intended to provide long-term regional direction, including where growth should occur and how infrastructure will support it, but the time frames for preparing those plans are really compressed, particularly given the need to align data across our entire region, infrastructure planning, and input from

multiple councils and agencies. And all of that increases the risk of incomplete information or inaccuracies. For example, spatial plans may signal growth in locations where infrastructure is not ready or even able to be funded. Those signals then flow through into the land use plans.

However, land use plans are only required to implement spatial plans, not be fully consistent with them. And there is no clear ability to decline a private plan change that then sits outside this framework. So in practice, development can still occur ahead of our infrastructure planning, and that's where you create a disconnect.

Councils are then required to respond by bringing infrastructure forward, often through borrowing, to service development that may not yet align with the planned sequence. And then you have the timing uncertainty. Land can be zoned and consented, including through fast track pathways, but development may not occur for several years or in the locations that best align with existing infrastructure capacity.

And then part of that is also potential land banking issues. So for councils, we're sitting there knowing that a development is coming, but not when. So we're having to make long-term infrastructure decisions ahead of any confirmed demand, and we're not actually seeing housing affordability improve.

That tells us that enabling supply alone may not be sufficient. More enabled land or long-term planning does not automatically translate into more houses being delivered in the short term, or indeed at lower prices, as we're seeing in Selwyn. Developers control release to manage risk and profitability, and rightly so. That's their business model.

But that can constrain supply, and that's the balance we're all trying to strike. Us here in Selwyn and you in Wellington. So we also want to make a comment on the regulatory relief and compensation provisions. There's a real risk that environmental protections delivered through these plans could create financial liabilities for councils without a clear funding mechanism.

So we either run roughshod over the environment, and then we don't have to pay compensation because the private landowner will be happy, but the people who live here have to live with those consequences, and those consequences are cost consequences to ratepayers. So when you combined all these factors, compressed time frames, weak alignment between the spatial and land use plans, timing uncertainty, and funding gaps, the likely outcome may not be the more enabling system that you're aiming for.

It may end up being more cautious. So our core point here in Selwyn is if councils are expected to deliver this, the system needs to be buildable, fundable, and properly aligned in practice. Otherwise, the risk and cost will continue to sit with the communities we represent in the form of continued rate increases.

Yeah. Thank you. Just want to add a few more things about the Natural Environment Bill as well. The limits do need to be nationally consistent and evidence-based. Take the point of the two submitters before, though, who mentioned around natural levels. That obviously can be taken into account.

We do not support no net loss of indigenous biodiversity in here, in Selwyn. Heavily modified environment already. If we lose any more, then there's going to be pretty much none. We do not support exemptions or directives to balance environmental against economic concerns. We're in a state where everything is already highly modified.

And particularly in our area, where we have nearly 2,000 kilometers worth of stock water races and land drainage, which are not defined as infrastructure, and therefore we cannot claim currently, or have them included in our development contributions policy. We would love to see natural infrastructure included in both of the bills, as something that we can then work with.

Because it has a huge impact when we have, for instance, a plan change which allows housing in an area which then creates flooding downstreamAnd lastly, interactions between all of these different documents, the policy statements and everything else. We've seen it before here where the national policy statement for urban development has different outcomes to highly productive land, and it's always the land that loses.

Somewhere along the line, the precedents that have already been set through the RMA, the common language that is understood by planners and developers and everybody else, we need some level of consistency and an understanding of how the hierarchy works, otherwise we are going to snarl the whole thing up in legal arguments and nothing will speed up.

It just will not be efficacious. So yeah, that's pretty much it. We've got about 30 seconds left, I think. Yes. Look, we have to cover- But thank you very much for the time... we'll go to Cameron Luxton first. Thank you. I'll just go back to an earlier part of your submission. You talked about Maori being reduced down to participation, and reading in your written submission, you've got council relationships with iwi required for consenting processes.

Is that currently the case, that iwi are required to have a say in consenting? And do you think that this new system will still enable the voice of iwi to be heard at the planning stage? And what's the justification for iwi having a say at the consenting stage?

In our local area, so the local iwi is Ngai Tahu, and the six nearby runanga, there's two of them that have their takiwā cover Selwyn District. They have an agency known as Mahonui Kurataio. We do invite them to put some input into a number of consents, depending on whether it's relevant to what exists in the iwi management plan.

At the moment, that works well from my personal point of view. What I'm worried about, and admittedly this isn't something I have personally discussed with the council, so this is my opinion on your question. If the law changes and requires a different level of participation from iwi Maori, then it will slowly degrade, being a death by a thousand cuts. We have good relationships right now, but if you aren't quite so compelled to have those relationships, the relationships will change over time because the power dynamic has changed.

Mm-hmm. That will be a loss. So to be clear, the function that's making Selwyn Council is the iwi partnership agreement to have say on consents. That was the answer to that. And then changing it would be a degrading of- There is value in hearing what iwi Maori have to say about- Oh, so for certain consents... consents that impact the environment, yeah.

Excellent. The iwi management plan is a stat... Well, I don't think it's a statutory document, but it's recognized in our district plan and in regional policy statements. Look, thank you very much. We're going to have to leave it there. We appreciate your time, your submission. Yeah, really good submission. Yeah, it was very helpful. So thank you. Thank you very much for that. Thank you for your time.

Thank you. Thank you. Good luck. So now moving on to our next submission, Save Our Springs Aotearoa New Zealand. Yes, please. Who are also joining us online. Yeah, nobody wants to drive over that road if they don't have to.

That's fair enough. Good morning, Kevin. Welcome to the select committee, and I can see you there, John, as well. You have 10 minutes with us, so take your submission as read, but we'll hand it over to you to talk us through it. So, thank you. Tena koutou, Chair. Tena koutou, members of committee.

Thank you for this opportunity to address you this morning. My name's Kevin Moore, and I am the coordinator of Save Our Springs. I'm joined by resource management lawyer, John Kamasaris. The purpose of Save Our Springs is to protect the aquifers, lakes, and rivers of New Zealand, particularly the pristine waters of Te Waihora Spring. We have over 3,000 supporters.

30,000. supporters. We submitted on the bills because we are deeply concerned about the degrading state of our water, and because we want the health of our waterways to improve. We are focused on water conservation orders because they protect the values of our most outstanding water bodies.

I will now hand over to John to take you through our submission in more detail. Kia ora. Thank you, Kevin, and good morning, Chair and committee members. Save Our Springs is very pleased to see water conservation orders retained in the Natural Environment Bill.

Since their inception, there have been a few special interest groups lobbying for water conservation orders to be scrapped because of perceived duplication and because of concerns about restrictions on farming. Save Our Springs strongly disagrees.

They are the only mechanism that recognizes and protects water bodies with nationally outstanding values. Water conservation orders recognize, in a nationally consistent way, the existing state of outstanding water bodies and provide limits to ensure those values are maintained.

They do not affect existing use rights-Or resource consents granted prior to commencement, as long as the activity remains the same. They do not override local planning processes. Rather, they are community led, robust and evidence-based, and the protection they provide is enduring.

They generally enable activities to continue so long as the existing state of the water body is conserved. And that is something, I would posit, many New Zealanders would agree is worth preserving in the new resource management system. Now, the Water Conservation Order provisions in the Natural Environment Bill largely mirror those in the RMA.

And I'll first touch on some of the improvements needed, and then address some of the amendments made in the bill, compared to the RMA that are both unnecessary and inappropriate. Starting first with the improvements, Save Our Springs acknowledges that the Water Conservation Order framework in the RMA is not perfect and even our most outstanding and protected water bodies are facing degradation.

This is a great opportunity for refinement. One particular area for improvement is, increasing the legal effect of Water Conservation Orders so that plans made under the bills have to give effect to the orders, and so that permits do not contravene the orders.

Another really important way that the orders can be improved is monitoring enforcement. Now, Save Our Springs supports the clarification that local authorities are responsible for monitoring, but further clarification in the bill is needed that consent authorities are also responsible for enforcing compliance.

Turning now to a couple of the amendments that have been made in the bills compared to the RMA, and these are discussed in detail in the written submissions, so I'll touch on them only briefly now. First is, Save Our Springs is saying that it's inappropriate that appeals to the Environment Court have been restricted to points of law only.

This is an important change because allowing appeals on factual issues is really important because it improves the rigor of the decision, and the Environment Court is the most appropriate place for those contentious, factual, merits-based issues to be considered.

Several Water Conservation Orders have gone to the Environment Court for final consideration, and that process has been robust and effective. The second change in the bill compared to the RMA that's of particular importance is the deletion of spiritual and cultural characteristics from the list of characteristics that Water Conservation Orders can protect.

Now, this is a significant change. There is no real reason for not including those characteristics, and their exclusion would leave a significant gap. It would exclude important relational and deeply human aspects of spirituality, such as sacredness, that may not otherwise be protected.

The Special Tribunal considering the Rangitata order, for instance, described the evidence from submitters about feelings and emotion they experienced of the river. And I'll just briefly read a passage that I think is apt. The tribunal said, "In addition to those who mentioned the spiritual values directly, others spoke of similar feelings, including the magic of the Rangitata River, the pure pleasure of being there, and how it has held a spell over their lives. They spoke of the peace, tranquility, the beauty and the grandeur, and the inherent wild and scenic qualities, especially in

the upper river. The wild turbulence of the white water in the gorge and the sheer volume of water in this river demands respect. The feeling of space and wideness of the sky at the mouth and the beautiful sunrises and sunsets were described."

Now, the most recent Water Conservation Order to Waikoropupu Springs is also an excellent example. Save Our Springs was intimately involved in the process to create that order and produced evidence to the Environment Court, including in respect of cultural and spiritual values.

Now to finish, I'll let Mr. Moran close with a short excerpt from the Court's decision, and then we are both happy to take questions. Yeah. Just to let you know, you only have a couple of minutes left of time, so if you want some questions, just to be mindful of that. Thank you. Yes. Thank you. I would just like to conclude my part in this with, just a short quote from the water conservation hearing, and this is part of the decision they made, and the judge and the two commissioners, this is quoting directly from the Water Conservation Order.

They said, "We accept the evidence of Mr. Moran and Ms. Samson on these matters. They explained how they find the springs to have a sacredness that is unique and spiritually renewing and hence outstanding. In essence, their experience was that the subject waters are extraordinary in those terms. These are, of course, personal experiences. Nevertheless, our site visits enabled us to appreciate how the special qualities of the springs include the way people experience them as spiritually rejuvenating and renewing."It is associated with the vibrancy

and purity of the waters and their peaceful setting. We adjudge that the springs have outstanding spiritual values. So I'll hand it back to you for questions. Thanks very much. We'll take the first question from Rachel. Thank you, and thanks for being clear that you are happy that WCOs are in the legislation, but not happy about the removal of the cultural and spiritual element and the need for, and the merits-based appeal.

Wanting to link to the fast track legislation, which doesn't account for WCOs and is still in play with these reforms, how do you see the link between that fast track and your concerns over the Sams Creek and then this legislation? Yes. So I'll start on that one. And I think, well, first of all, it's clear that water conservation orders need to also apply to fast track applications.

Fast track approvals should not be able to be granted where they're contravening a water conservation order. The orders are essentially the national park of water bodies in New Zealand, and we have similar restrictions in relation to mining for Schedule 4 land under the Conservation Act. The same should apply in respect of water conservation orders.

Kevin, did you have anything to add to that, or are we able to take another question? Yeah, I think we can take another question, Shauna. Yep. Okay. Cameron, you didn't have a question? Okay. No. We- I've got a question. Oh, Lan. Yes. Tena koutou. Thank you so much for your submission.

My question was about the legislation generally. We've heard there's, to put it in maybe a more positive note, a lot of potential gaps when it comes to environmental protection generally. And I was wondering, a number of people have raised the potential for something like a maintain or improve clause in the legislation that would sort of essentially just hold the line and ideally make things get better.

Do you think that's something that could be in place to actually protect not only water conservation orders, but special areas like Te Waipoupou? And would that have value in your opinion? Yes, of course it would. And just to note that water conservation orders are about conservation, and you would've heard a lot about, from certain groups, about water conservation orders restricting activities and being really about improving things. And actually, a lot of the time, it's just about maintaining the existing state of these outstanding water

bodies. And so, of course, there is a gap in relation to actually improvement. And that is something that should be addressed and provided for in the bills. But it's also just to note that the orders themselves, they provide for conservation of outstanding values and as I've said in the submission, I think that's something that a lot of Kiwis would and do support.

Look, thank you very much. We are gonna have to leave it there. We've gone over time, but we appreciate your time today, and your submission. So thank you. Thank you. So we now move to our next submitter, Groundswell New Zealand.

Oh, there we go. We can see you there, Bryce. Welcome to the-- and Jamie, we can see you there. Hello. Welcome to the select committee today. Look, you've got 10 minutes with us for your submission, but take your submission as read, and we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your main points. Thank you. Okay. Thank you. I'll just introduce myself. I'm Bryce McKenzie.

I don't know if Laurie Patterson's here. I can't see anybody else on the screen. He was going to be here. Him and I were the co-founders of Groundswell, and we have Jamie McFadyen, who is our environmental spokesman, who will be doing most of the speaking. But perhaps, if I can just start off by saying we were pretty disappointed that with an email base of over 80,000 and a massive social media following, that we missed the cut to be able to do a decent presentation. This seems pretty short-sighted, when we consider other groups representing agriculture had at least

three hours, and possibly longer, with having input into the bill initially. So we're pretty disappointed in that. But however, here we are. So, for a coalition government who campaigned on removing regulations, we have some suggestions for you on how to do that. For myself, I'm a member of the award-winning, nationally recognized Pomahaka Water Care group. We're a group of volunteers who have achieved over 50% of people in our catchment being members of our group.

Now, this is just done by encouraging people to take an interest in their catchment. What we've done is, basically, we started water testing back in 2015 to understand where we needed to put our energies. Once we found out what our main problems were, the next step was to, of course, work out how we could rectify those problemsNow, we've managed to come a long way in that time and have won a number of awards for what we've done.

One of the other things we did which we found worked really well is we started up a best practice response team, and they have their own hotline. Now, that receives many calls and is followed up by members of the Watercare group. And we get people reporting any breaches of best practice, and it is well used.

One question we do have, and we've discussed it a bit, is why a catchment with good water quality needs to have bureaucratic farm plans which give far more stress for no real gain. So if you want to get rid of some regulations, we have some suggestions for it, and I'll pass it over to Jamie to follow up.

Okay, I'm just going to go through some of the key points in our submission. And we started off with the international research from out of the UK, and just some key points from that. And it was around the dramatic rise of local-led restoration across the world as people have lost faith in the state's ability to provide environmental leadership- Oh, you're breaking up a bit there, Jamie.

We're struggling to hear you... suitable legislation. What we're seeing is top-down approaches across the world are increasing from that research, the buy-in and empowerment of local... Can you hear me all right? Yeah, we're... Yeah, better now. Better now? Yes. Yeah. So the third point was that the buy-in and empowerment of local people and communities in environmental restoration is not just a nice to have, but a must to achieve success.

The second point I just wanted to make, it's useful to understand the context I bring to the submission. So I'm a hill country sheep and beef farmer. I was one of the first students in 1989 to finish a three-year university course that included the RMA. Three years of environmental services chair on Hurunui District Council and qualified as a hearings commissioner.

I bring a perspective of working over the past 30 years with hundreds of farmers, advising and implementing environmental projects from the high country mountains to the sea. I live and breathe the environment, the RMA consents, farm plans and practical farming in a way that few others experience. So my third point, before the RMA, farmers had government subsidies to clear forests, drain wetlands and maximize stock numbers.

Despite this, many chose an alternative path, the catchment board system of a trusted partnership of an advisor working with farmers through a rolling farm environment plan that blended conservation with development. The legacy being extensive tree plantings, farmers setting up the QEII Trust, and 2.8 million hectares of native vegetation on sheep and beef farms, a quarter of our country's total native vegetation.

Where else in the world can such an impressive claim be made? And previous submitters have talked about the failure of farm plans. Well, that's simply not true. The success of the catchment board farm plan system was it empowered farmers in environmental sustainability. So that was a huge successful programme.

Point four, with the RMA, we relied on rules and consents to manage development. We do not argue that regulation is definitely needed for the likes of water takes, effluent ponds, discharges, many other activities. But relying solely on regulation to protect the environment has proven disastrous under the RMA.

20 years ago, with the dairy boom in full swing, we saw a rapid loss of the remaining Canterbury Plains' dryland forest remnants, wetlands, and the pollution of rivers. I approached the irrigation and dairy sectors in North Canterbury, drew up a plan and set about to successfully implement a system similar to the catchment board. It is no fluke that our area of North Canterbury is now seen as environmental leaders with a huge amount of restoration and enhancement, particularly wetlands and waterways.

And the stuff we're doing at the moment is just, it's incredible, I mean, the amount of work we're doing. Point five, section six, particularly SNA's Landscapes, and more recently SASMs, has been a curse on our country, pitting residents against their councils with protests from Northland to Southland over the life of the RMA.

The regulatory relief proposal adds more administration complexity and cost and doesn't deal with the central issue. Section six is inherently bad law and needs to go. How can you achieve protection enhancement of natural and cultural values if the planning system penalizes those proactive environmental endeavor and turns natural and cultural values into a liability?

Freshwater farm plans. No matter how you shape this, many farmers will be worse off under this incredibly ill-conceived policy. It goes against everything this current government promises of reducing red tape, bureaucracy, government oversight and cost. It is particularly galling given the huge amount of industry and grassroots effort going into many different initiatives, including farm environment plans.

Groundswell NZ and grassroots farmers support integrated holistic farm environment plans, not ones just focused on freshwaterAnd we support these as one of the tools in the toolbox, but they must be industry-led and a top-down government approach will be met with fierce resistance and will fail.

I have been actively involved in many farm plan models over 30 years. My two most important messages are, one, that every farmer and farm is different, and the mix of approaches and tools that work best for each farmer is incredibly varied. One tool, one size fits all doesn't work.

Point two, the key outcome with farm plans is that it must empower farmers and environmental sustainability, that they live and breathe in everything they do. Question, 83% of farmers in a beef and lamb survey oppose mandatory government prescribed freshwater farm plans.

How do you expect this policy to succeed with this degree of opposition? Consents. Massive issues with consents, and it's the cost. Many of our clients, $30,000 to $80,000, taking two to four years for irrigation consent renewals. Previous submitter talked about the role of iwi in consents, and it's just made it more complex and more frustrating for farmers.

Seven, point seven: How many catchment type groups, biodiversity trust, weed and pest initiatives are across New Zealand? How many dairy and irrigating farmers are already under a farm environment plan system? Section 32 of the RMA is an excellent planning mechanism, not given genuine attention by councils or central government.

How can you develop workable environment legislation if you don't know the current state? Hence, Groundswell NZ's submission that a stocktake of current state is done before progressing the legislation any further. This is also to assess what the threats are to our environment, particularly in weeds and pests.

Point eight, last point. Groundswell NZ is incorrectly seen as a protest movement against regulation. Bryce McKenzie's founding principle remains someone at the forefront of environmental sustainability, Pomahaka Water Care Group, frustrated with the failure of government to not only design workable legislation, but that undermines the environmental work of landowners and communities. Thank you.

Look, thank you very much, Jamie, and a lovely background that you have there on the farm. Look, we don't have time for many questions, but we'll go to Grant McCallum for a question. Gidday, team. Thanks for your submission. Really appreciate it. I'm just curious, you talk about section six and the whole SNA concept. How as farming community, how do you then reassure the general public of New Zealand that actually any areas of real natural significance, like particular wetlands, will be taken care of if there's no regulatory oversight?

How could we actually achieve that? So the first thing is that the rules and the SNA mapping don't protect the SNAs. So we did a presentation to our local council of a whole heap of SNAs around here, like this one behind me. Yeah. And we demonstrated that the rules and mapping were not protecting these SNAs because the protection of SNAs relies on active management, and that active management has to come from farmers.

So what they've done in our district is a template for the rest of the country. They got rid of SNAs. There's no mapped SNAs. We've got a biodiversity trust. I'm a founding trustee. We've got the Hurunui District Land Care Group. We've got catchment groups, a whole lot of new catchment groups. We've got a whole lot of biodiversity initiatives being rolled out by the community. The council have stepped back and let us get on with it.

They support us and they've let us get on with it. You've got to remove natural and cultural values from the legislation, otherwise you'll keep having them as a liability. They've got to be an asset. Thank you. Just Grant, if I could say something about it, particularly addressing wetlands. The wetlands in our catchment have grown, I would hate to think by numerically how many, because when we did trials, we found the only way to remove phosphorus from waterways was to actually be able to stop the water flow and let it drop out. So our wetlands in the

Pomahaka catchment, I would say have probably increased tenfold. So it's just happening naturally if you have an interest in the purity of your water. Thank you. Look. Thank you. Thank you, and just time for one last quick question. Rachel Brooking. Just tiny question on the photos you've got there and the willows. And I understand the problems with crack willows, but you're talking about the purple willows as well. On this photo, are these baby willows or are the willows further out?

I didn't mention this, but this is probably a critical point. I was in a meeting with regional council and a whole lot of ecologists and others, and my gosh, amongst us, we think this willow weed is the biggest threat we've ever seen in terms of weeds of pest in New Zealand. It's taking over whole braid plains of riverbeds.

I had regional council staff, senior staff out there a couple of weeks ago. Couldn't believe it. Whole braid plains. So the crack river grows on the sides of the willows, of the rivers. It doesn't grow through the middles of the braid plains that much. Whereas this willow, it's a purple willow, it's a shrub type willow. It's taken over whole braid plains of riverbeds.

It's absolutely frightening. It's this low stuff on the... It's the- Yeah. It's all the low stuff in the middle of the photo. So on the left, far left and the far right, you can probably just see some crack willows. Yeah. But all through the middle of that photo, that used to be clear braid plain where river nesting birds used to nest. So that's why we say with the whole legislation, the legislation's almost, we're so far behind what we're dealing with at the coal face here.

We're dealing with massive environmental disasters here. Deer and pigs like on this farm here. It's just out of control, and we've got legislation that's not getting us in front of all this environmental damage that we're seeing. Question. Okay. Thank you very much, Jamie, Bryce, and Laurie.
We appreciate your time today. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you. So that brings us, members, to the end of our public session, and so we will now move into private session.
